comment_text.sn12.12:1.1: In this discourse, “ignorance”, “choices”, and “name and form” are not mentioned directly, but they are, as I note below, implicit.
comment_text.sn12.12:2.1: This monk appears in three discourses, in none of which he distinguishes himself. At MN 21 he is reprimanded for being too friendly with nuns. And at SN 12.32 Venerable Sāriputta gets word that Phagguna had disrobed. According to the commentary, he was called Phagguna “of the Top-Knot” because of the style he wore his hair as a layperson. The name stuck, presumably to distinguish him from the Phagguna of AN 6.56 (and probably SN 35.83) who died in robes as a non-returner after hearing a Dhamma talk.
comment_text.sn12.12:2.2: Evoking the mysterious Creator ka (“who?”) of Rig Veda 121, Phagguna assumes an agent who acts, which of course would be the Self. This leans on the feature of Pali and Sanskrit whereby the agent of a verb need not be stated but can be assumed. This lends itself to the notion that a hidden agent underlies the act of creation.
comment_text.sn12.12:2.11: Here “rebirth into a new state of existence in the future” occupies the link between consciousness and the six senses, where we normally find “choices” and “name and form”. The being who has been reborn is referred to as bhūta. For “name and form” as the individual growing from embryo to adulthood, see DN 15:21.2).
comment_text.sn12.12:6.11: The Pali text omits rebirth, etc. The commentary says Phagguna stopped asking questions here, offering three quite different reasons why. A simpler explanation would be that the text is abbreviated.
comment_text.sn12.12:7.1: Here, cessation starts with the six senses (as at SN 12.24:21.9).

root_text.sn12.12:1.2: “Cattārome, bhikkhave, āhārā bhūtānaṁ vā sattānaṁ ṭhitiyā sambhavesīnaṁ vā anuggahāya.
root_text.sn12.12:1.4: Kabaḷīkāro āhāro oḷāriko vā sukhumo vā, phasso dutiyo, manosañcetanā tatiyā, viññāṇaṁ catutthaṁ.
root_text.sn12.12:1.5: Ime kho, bhikkhave, cattāro āhārā bhūtānaṁ vā sattānaṁ ṭhitiyā sambhavesīnaṁ vā anuggahāyā”ti.
root_text.sn12.12:2.1: Evaṁ vutte, āyasmā moḷiyaphagguno bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn12.12:2.8: Evaṁ maṁ avadantaṁ yo evaṁ puccheyya:
root_text.sn12.12:2.9: ‘kissa nu kho, bhante, viññāṇāhāro’ti, esa kallo pañho.
root_text.sn12.12:2.11: ‘viññāṇāhāro āyatiṁ punabbhavābhinibbattiyā paccayo, tasmiṁ bhūte sati saḷāyatanaṁ, saḷāyatanapaccayā phasso’”ti.
root_text.sn12.12:3.7: Evaṁ maṁ avadantaṁ yo evaṁ puccheyya:
root_text.sn12.12:3.8: ‘kiṁpaccayā nu kho, bhante, phasso’ti, esa kallo pañho.
root_text.sn12.12:4.7: Evaṁ maṁ avadantaṁ yo evaṁ puccheyya:
root_text.sn12.12:4.8: ‘kiṁpaccayā nu kho, bhante, vedanā’ti, esa kallo pañho.
root_text.sn12.12:5.7: Evaṁ maṁ avadantaṁ yo evaṁ puccheyya:
root_text.sn12.12:5.8: ‘kiṁpaccayā nu kho, bhante, taṇhā’ti, esa kallo pañho.
root_text.sn12.12:6.7: Evaṁ maṁ avadantaṁ yo evaṁ puccheyya:
root_text.sn12.12:6.8: ‘kiṁpaccayā nu kho, bhante, upādānan’ti, esa kallo pañho.
root_text.sn12.12:7.1: Channaṁ tveva, phagguna, phassāyatanānaṁ asesavirāganirodhā phassanirodho;

translation_text.sn12.12:1.2: “Mendicants, these four fuels maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those about to be born.
translation_text.sn12.12:1.4: Edible food, whether solid or subtle; contact is the second, mental intention the third, and consciousness the fourth.
translation_text.sn12.12:1.5: These are the four fuels that maintain sentient beings that have been born and help those that are about to be born.”
translation_text.sn12.12:2.1: When he said this, Venerable Phagguna of the Top-Knot said to the Buddha,
translation_text.sn12.12:2.2: “But sir, who consumes consciousness as fuel?”
translation_text.sn12.12:2.3: “That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha.
translation_text.sn12.12:2.4: “I don’t speak of one who consumes.
translation_text.sn12.12:2.5: If I were to speak of one who consumes, then it would be cogent to ask
translation_text.sn12.12:2.7: But I don’t speak like that.
translation_text.sn12.12:2.8: Hence it would be cogent to ask:
translation_text.sn12.12:2.10: And a cogent answer to this would be:
translation_text.sn12.12:2.11: ‘Consciousness as fuel is a requirement for regeneration into a new state of existence in the future. When the being is present, there are the six sense fields. The six sense fields are requirements for contact.’”
translation_text.sn12.12:3.2: “That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha.
translation_text.sn12.12:3.3: “I don’t speak of one who contacts.
translation_text.sn12.12:3.4: If I were to speak of one who contacts, then it would be cogent to ask
translation_text.sn12.12:3.6: But I don’t speak like that.
translation_text.sn12.12:3.7: Hence it would be cogent to ask:
translation_text.sn12.12:3.9: And a cogent answer to this would be:
translation_text.sn12.12:3.10: ‘The six sense fields are requirements for contact. Contact is a requirement for feeling.’”
translation_text.sn12.12:4.2: “That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha.
translation_text.sn12.12:4.3: “I don’t speak of one who feels.
translation_text.sn12.12:4.4: If I were to speak of one who feels, then it would be cogent to ask
translation_text.sn12.12:4.6: But I don’t speak like that.
translation_text.sn12.12:4.7: Hence it would be cogent to ask:
translation_text.sn12.12:4.9: And a cogent answer to this would be:
translation_text.sn12.12:4.10: ‘Contact is a requirement for feeling. Feeling is a requirement for craving.’”
translation_text.sn12.12:5.2: “That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha.
translation_text.sn12.12:5.3: “I don’t speak of one who craves.
translation_text.sn12.12:5.4: If I were to speak of one who craves, then it would be cogent to ask
translation_text.sn12.12:5.6: But I don’t speak like that.
translation_text.sn12.12:5.7: Hence it would be cogent to ask:
translation_text.sn12.12:5.9: And a cogent answer to this would be:
translation_text.sn12.12:5.10: ‘Feeling is a requirement for craving. Craving is a requirement for grasping.’”
translation_text.sn12.12:6.2: “That’s not a cogent question,” said the Buddha.
translation_text.sn12.12:6.3: “I don’t speak of one who grasps.
translation_text.sn12.12:6.4: If I were to speak of one who grasps, then it would be cogent to ask
translation_text.sn12.12:6.6: But I don’t speak like that.
translation_text.sn12.12:6.7: Hence it would be cogent to ask:
translation_text.sn12.12:6.9: And a cogent answer to this would be:
translation_text.sn12.12:6.11: Grasping is a requirement for continued existence.’ …
translation_text.sn12.12:6.12: That is how this entire mass of suffering originates.
translation_text.sn12.12:7.1: When the six fields of contact fade away and cease with nothing left over, contact ceases.
translation_text.sn12.12:7.5: When grasping ceases, continued existence ceases.
translation_text.sn12.12:7.6: When continued existence ceases, rebirth ceases.
translation_text.sn12.12:7.7: When rebirth ceases, old age and death, sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress cease.
translation_text.sn12.12:7.8: That is how this entire mass of suffering ceases.”
